10. Neural Information Retrieval (Similarity-Based LR) (Omar Khattab)

Learning representations allows for improving generalization and trasnfer learning, but another goal is to organize information by exploiting geometric similarity.

10.1. Information retrieval (IR)

  • Information retrieval (1950s): You have a corpus of N docs. Want to build a system for search and retrieval, and return the top-k relevant documents.

    • Some metrics: success: if at least one relevant result is in top-K. Precision: number of relevant items in top K over K. Recall: number of relevant items in top K over total number of relevant items.
    • You want sub-linear search time.
  • Deep learning beat googole keyword search in 2019 with pretraining transformers.
  • Idea 1 (Classifier) for modelling IR as a deep learning prolbem: output logits based on a transformer encoder + CLS token. Model is too damn big. Problem is that the representations are not built on top of the content of the documents. You would need a lot of labels for each document.
  • Idea 2 (Joint encoder): Add query + documents to transformer and map each document’s last token to a score. Problem is that cost kills you.

  • Idea 3: Decomposition/Cross-Encoders (pointwise attention: Assume that document scores are easy to compute accurately in an independent way. Hence we can get a score from running attention between a query and a document.

    • Ask humans to label 1-2 “relevant” documents for each query. Assume everything else is irrelevant.
    • For each q, sample a single d and train a linear layer output hte transformer to map output vector to a score.
    • Runs in linear time for each query.

    • If we have a fast method to get top 10 K then we can use this to get the top K. The fast method is with dual-encoders (weaker than cross-encoders):

      • Make another assumption: assume the document representation doesn’t depend on the query, i.e. decompose the score function into a dot product between the query and document embeddings similarity. We can precompute document representations and so at query time we need only one transformer forward pass.

      • [TODO] Contrastive learning: train with InfoNCE loss. Based on sampling labeled (q, d+, d-) triples

        • [TODO] total negativies
        • See later explanation on it

  • We can get sublinear via indexing: cluster all document vectors into groups via k-means (assuming clustering goes well). At query time: probe with representative vectors of each cluster and then go within each cluster. This is .

    • This is an algorithm for approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search

10.2. Dual encoder Approximation and generalization

  • Limitations of a single dense vector representation. It is skeptical that you can represent an article as a 2000 dimensional vector.
  • Under mild assumptions, a dual encoder can approximate any continuous scoring function with large enough embedding dimension and encoder (Menon et al. 2022)
  • Unfortunately, for any dimesnion , it is easy to define a retrieval task where is too small empirically and theoretically (Weller et al. 2025)

  • In practice, we often see that these methods are indeed expressive enough—we see low error on the training set—but poor generalization. Cross attention generalizes much better.

  • Intuition: blunt (low resolution) representations lead to blunt updates. The query vector is shared across all documents. To make q farther from a document, you might move it to other negative documents. Flaw in inductive bias for dual encoders.

10.3. Late interaction paradigm

  • Can we get the best of the two worlds?

  • Late interaction paradigm: Main value of attention for IR is the fine-grained interactions. “Late interaction” means the query–document interaction happens after encoding, but before the final aggregation — hence, late relative to encoding, early relative to scoring. Represent a document and query as multiple tokens. Compute the score as the maximum similarity of any with , summed over the query tokens. In particular, each word is a token and we run it through BERT to get a good encoding. Previously we encoded documents as single vectors based on pooling (CLS token or attention) on the output tokens produced by BERT encoder.
  • Combine this with ANN indexing to get fast speed
